sections.question: As you know, I am Treasurer for the Corman Campaign Committee. The Committee is now considering contributing to a general Democratic mailer being prepared by Alexander Pope. The contemplated mailer will contain statements from various candidates with a general statement of issues, etc. Our contribution would be limited to the cost of the page with respect to the Corman Campaign and the pro rata cost of the statement of issues page. I am under the impression that Statutory requirements promulgated under Proposition 9 do not require reporting of such contributions inasmuch as they are not direct contributions to a candidate. Would you please issue an opinion letter confirming that no reporting will be required by the Corman campaign Committee and further indicate whether the mailer must contain a statement that a portion thereof pertaining to Congressman Corman was paid for by the Corman campaign Committee.
sections.conclusion: If the Corman Committee receives full and adequate consideration (paying only its proportional cost), it will not have made a contribution to Pope. The Committee must be identified on the mailer in the manner specified in Section 84035 only if it is paying the largest portion of the mailer's costs. Payments received by the Corman Committee will be 'contributions' if donors know or have reason to know they will be used for contributions to state or local candidates, and must be apportioned reasonably. The Committee will qualify as a recipient committee requiring registration only if it receives $500 or more in contributions, though only that portion allocable to state and local candidate contributions counts toward this threshold.
sections.question_synthetic: The Corman Campaign Committee seeks to contribute to a shared Democratic mailer prepared by Alexander Pope by paying for its own page and a pro rata share of a general issues statement. The requestor asks whether this requires reporting under the Political Reform Act and whether the mailer must identify the Corman Committee as a funding source.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: If the Corman Committee pays only its proportional share of the mailer cost, it has not made a reportable contribution to Pope and need only be identified if it is the largest funder. Payments the Committee receives will be 'contributions' subject to apportionment based on the percentage of its expenditures allocated to state and local candidates; only amounts above $500 (allocated to such contributions) trigger registration and reporting requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated September 19, 1978 but appears to be misfiled as shown at end) and the FPPC response letter dated September 29, 1979. Some OCR errors present but content is generally clear. Document ID date (1978) does not match letter date (1979); letter references incoming request of September 19, 1978. Extracted primarily from the FPPC response portion as the primary document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As you know, I am Treasurer for the Corman Campaign Committee. The Committee is now considering contributing to a general Democratic mailer being prepared by Alexander Pope. The contemplated mailer will contain statements from various candidates with a general statement of issues, etc. Our contribution would be limited to the cost of the page with respect to the Corman Campaign and the pro rata cost of the statement of issues page. I am under the impression that Statutory requirements promulgated under Proposition 9 do not require reporting of such contributions inasmuch as they are not direct contributions to a candidate. Would you please issue an opinion letter confirming that no reporting will be required by the Corman campaign Committee and further indicate whether the mailer must contain a statement that a portion thereof pertaining to Congressman Corman was paid for by the Corman campaign Committee.

CONCLUSION: If the Corman Committee receives full and adequate consideration (paying only its proportional cost), it will not have made a contribution to Pope. The Committee must be identified on the mailer in the manner specified in Section 84035 only if it is paying the largest portion of the mailer's costs. Payments received by the Corman Committee will be 'contributions' if donors know or have reason to know they will be used for contributions to state or local candidates, and must be apportioned reasonably. The Committee will qualify as a recipient committee requiring registration only if it receives $500 or more in contributions, though only that portion allocable to state and local candidate contributions counts toward this threshold.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the Corman Campaign Committee that purchasing a page in a shared Democratic mailer by paying its proportional cost does not constitute a reportable contribution to candidate Pope, provided adequate consideration is received and identification requirements are met. The response also addresses the Committee's filing obligations based on contributions it receives from donors, establishing an apportionment method for determining which portions of payments constitute reportable contributions.
